mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar S-Type engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S-Type engine typically costs £4,200–£9,500 supply-only, depending on the engine variant [JAG-QUOTE]. The 3.0 V6 is the cheapest to replace at £4,500–£7,500; the 4.2 supercharged V8 is the most expensive at £6,000–£9,500. Labour adds 10–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent specialist) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Use our diagnostic calculator to get a personalised estimate for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: Which S-Type engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0 V6 (AJ126) has the cleanest reliability record in our data — it's the simplest engine in the range (no supercharger, no timing chain tensioner issues as severe as the V8s, and a well-understood cooling system). The 2.5 V6 is similarly reliable but less common; the 4.2 V8 is good from 2004 onward but carries higher costs when it does fail.

mainEntity.name: Which S-Type engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The pre-2002 4.0 V8 (AJ-V8) carries the highest risk — its Nikasil cylinder liners were vulnerable to high-sulphur UK fuel, and many affected cars suffered bore wear, misfires and compression loss. Any 4.0 V8 without documented cylinder health checks is a risk. The pre-2004 4.2 V8 (AJ133) also carries a timing chain tensioner risk — the 2004 revision largely resolved it. Avoid any S-Type V8 without documented history of a timing chain/tensioner inspection.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my S-Type engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Common symptoms by engine: V6 (AJ126/AJ-V6): Overheating from coolant leaks, water pump failure, or head-gasket failure (from ignored overheating). White smoke or persistent coolant loss is a warning sign. V8 (AJ133): Rattling on cold start (timing chain tensioners), overheating from coolant crossover pipe failure, loss of power (supercharger coupler wear on R models). 4.0 V8 (AJ-V8): Misfires, poor compression, oil consumption — all signs of Nikasil bore wear. Get a compression test before buying any pre-2002 4.0.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar S-Type engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on the car's value versus the replacement cost. A £4,500–£7,500 recon 3.0 V6 on a £4,000–£5,000 car is borderline — at the 60% threshold, you need to think hard. A £6,000–£9,500 recon 4.2 supercharged V8 on a £3,000 S-Type R is almost never worth it unless the car is a future classic with sentimental value. As a rule, if replacement cost exceeds 60% of the car's current value, get a scrap valuation before committing.

The 2004–2008 3.0 V6 SE with full service history is the sweet spot — the later facelift gives you the revised cooling system and interior upgrades, the V6 is the most reliable engine, and SE trim gets you the equipment you actually want (leather, climate, decent audio) without the complexity of the V8s. Avoid pre-2002 4.0 V8s; buy a 4.2 V8 only if you're prepared for higher running costs.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my S-Type engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your S-Type's engine code is on the VIN plate (inside the driver's door frame or on the windscreen base) and also stamped on the engine block itself. For the V6 engines, look near the front of the block; for V8s, check the passenger-side cylinder head area. If you're still unsure, our diagnostic calculator can help you identify it from your registration or VIN.

mainEntity.name: S-Type vs X-Type — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S-Type (particularly the 3.0 V6) is more reliable than the X-Type — but that's a low bar. The X-Type shares Ford Mondeo-derived underpinnings and carries a 🔴 Scrap Threshold flag in our data: replacement cost frequently exceeds vehicle value. The S-Type's engineering is more Jaguar-heritage (V6/V8, rear-wheel-drive, the traditional Jaguar layout) and it has a stronger enthusiast network. That said, both are ageing cars; the S-Type is the one we'd trust more — but we'd buy a post-2004 example and budget for cooling system work.

hero.h1: S-Type Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every S-Type engine from the 2.5 V6 to the 4.2 V8 and the legendary R — 1999–2008 production, 70+ real UK enquiries, verified costs, and an honest verdict on whether replacement still makes financial sense [JAG-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: 🔴 The S-Type rule: check the car's value before the repair quote — a £4,500+ recon engine on a £2,500 car rarely makes sense.
AJ126 3.0 V6 — the simplest and most widely supported S-Type engine; fewer weak points than the V8.
AJ133 4.2 Supercharged V8 — timing chain tensioner wear and cooling system complexity; expensive to repair correctly.
Late 2004–2008 3.0 V6 SE with full service history — the sweet spot between performance and running costs.
Cooling system leaks and overheating — AJ126/AJ133 shared weakness [PATTERN DATA]
⚠️ Classic Value Check — production ended; value assessment essential before committing to replacement

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The S-Type is an ageing, characterful Jaguar that rewards careful buying and regular maintenance — but as a repair proposition, the moment the engine fails, you must evaluate the car's value honestly before agreeing to a quote.

rows.value: Petrol only (no diesel S-Type was sold in the UK)
AJ133 4.2 Supercharged V8 (pre-2004 timing chain tensioners)

generations.subHeadline: The S-Type ran from 1999–2008 across two distinct engineering eras — the early Jaguar-developed V6/V8 and the later Ford-era refinements.

cards.title: X200 Generation
cards.verdict: The S-Type is a single-generation car with two distinct halves: pre-2002 cars have the earlier Nikasil-lined V8s that can suffer bore wear; post-2004 cars gain the revised 4.2 V8 with better reliability. Buy the facelift, avoid the very earliest V8s unless the engine has been replaced.

cards.title: Facelift Matters — The S-Type's Real Dividing Line
cards.meta: The S-Type's engineering story splits cleanly at the 2002 facelift (2004 model year). Pre-2002: the 4.0 V8 with Nikasil liners (a known bore-wear risk in UK cars on high-sulphur fuel). Post-2004: the revised 4.2 V8 (AJ133 family) with steel liners and better timing chain tensioner design — the engine Jaguar should have built from the start.

comparisonLink.label: Read the full comparison: Pre-2002 vs Post-2004 S-Type →

engineDatabase.h2: S-Type Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the 1999–2008 Jaguar S-Type, verified against Jaguar factory specifications.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six issues S-Type owners report most frequently — some engine-specific, some shared with the wider Jaguar family.

problems.description: Early 4.0 V8 engines (1999–2002) used Nikasil cylinder liners that could be damaged by high-sulphur UK fuel — causing loss of compression and misfires. Most affected cars are now off the road or have had replacement engines.
The earlier 4.2 V8's timing chain tensioners — same issue as the XK8/XKR — can fail without warning. Jaguar revised the design from 2004 onward. Pre-2004 cars warrant immediate inspection.

problems.issue: AJ126 / AJ133 Cooling System Crossover Pipe Failure
problems.description: Plastic coolant crossover pipes become brittle with age and crack — common across both V6 and V8 S-Types. Overheating can cause head-gasket failure if ignored.
The S-Type's air suspension (especially the rear self-levelling system on V8 models) can fail due to compressor burnout or air spring leaks. A common non-engine failure pattern.
The 2.5 and 3.0 V6 water pump can fail with age — a well-documented weakness on the AJ-V6 family. Replacement before failure is recommended at 60k–80k miles.

The S-Type's era of electronics — instrument cluster failures, central locking module issues and battery drain — typical of early-2000s Jaguars and the source of the brand's older "electrical" reputation.

marketIntelligence.h2: S-Type Market Intelligence — What 70+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: "Interesting insight": The S-Type's 70 enquiries in 2025 place it well below modern Jaguars like the XF (454+ combined enquiries) and XE (260) — a clear sign that the car has moved into classic/enthusiast ownership rather than daily-driver territory, where engine replacements are less frequent because owners either maintain them carefully or scrap them when major failure occurs.

The S-Type's enquiry volume is actually higher than the older XJ X300 (22) and XJ X308 (32), but significantly lower than the more modern XJ X351 (151) — the car sits in a "classic-but-not-yet-collectible" sweet spot where ownership costs still matter to buyers.

The 3.0 V6 is the most-enquired engine, reflecting that it was the UK's best-selling S-Type powertrain. The V8s collectively generate a similar volume of enquiries, but the V6's popularity makes it the most-supported engine in the specialist network.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every S-Type Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you're buying an S-Type, buy a 2004-onward 3.0 V6 with full service history, and budget for a cooling system refresh within the first year. If you already own one and the engine fails, get a realistic valuation before you agree to a rebuild — a £5,000 quote on a £2,500 car is a decision you need to make with your eyes open. End of Sections 1-7 (S-Type). Ready for Sections 8-14. JAGUAR S-TYPE — SECTIONS 8-14

engineEvolution.h2: How the S-Type Engine Range Evolved — 1999 to 2008

eras.whyBmwChanged: S-Type launched as Jaguar's entry-level saloon under Ford ownership. The V8 was the flagship engine, but the V6s were the volume sellers.
eras.worthKnowing: The 4.0 V8 used Nikasil cylinder liners — a material that proved vulnerable to high-sulphur UK fuel. Many 4.0 S-Types were replaced under warranty; survivors are rare.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Jaguar phased out the 4.0 V8 and its problematic Nikasil liners, replacing it with the 4.2 AJ133 — a steel-lined engine with revised timing chain tensioners and improved cooling.
eras.worthKnowing: The pre-2004 4.2 V8 still carried the older chain tensioner design; 2004-on cars got the revised, more durable tensioners — the key buying threshold.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The S-Type R launched in 2002 with the 4.2 supercharged V8, but the 2004 facelift refined the engine bay and cooling systems across the range.
eras.worthKnowing: The supercharged 4.2 shares the same core reliability profile as the naturally aspirated 4.2 — but the supercharger itself adds a known "coupler rattle" weakness and more complex servicing.

profiles.verdict: The S-Type's boot is generous and rear-seat space is adequate, but rear headroom is tight for taller adults and the running costs (especially V8 fuel economy) make it an expensive family car. A diesel XF or Mondeo is a better family buy.
A 3.0 V6 with full service history is a comfortable, refined commuter — but 22–26 mpg and rising specialist costs mean it's not a budget-friendly daily. Buy one as a second car, not your only car.

The S-Type is cheap to buy (£1,500–£3,000 buys a usable example) but expensive to keep. A £4,500 engine replacement on a £2,000 car is a financial mistake. If your budget is tight, buy a well-maintained V6 and accept that one major failure means scrapping it.

The S-Type R's 4.2 supercharged V8 offers genuine performance for under £8,000 — but you're buying a future classic, not a daily driver. Budget for cooling system refresh, supercharger coupler replacement, and timing chain inspection.

If you're buying your first Jaguar, the X-Type is cheaper to run and the XF X260 is safer to own. The S-Type is a car for someone who already knows they want a classic-era Jaguar — not a first step into the brand.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My S-Type Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Sub-headline: The S-Type's economics are unique — a 3.0 V6 and a 4.2 Supercharged V8 sit at opposite ends of the repair-vs-replace spectrum. Let's work out which one you're dealing with.

Path 2 — Not sure which engine you have? We'll identify it first

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range on this page comes from genuine UK enquiry data and Jaguar specialist quotes — 70+ S-Type enquiries [JAG-VERIFIED] in 2025 alone, cross-referenced against real Jaguar specialist cost sheets.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: When a £4,500–£7,500 reconditioned engine exceeds 60% of your S-Type's market value, we say so — and we tell you to get a scrap valuation alongside your repair quote. That's the difference between a knowledge base and a sales page.

signals.title: Vetted Jaguar Specialists
signals.text: Our network includes 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists who work on S-Types daily — not generalists who happen to take on Jaguar jobs. They know the Nikasil bore wear, the timing chain tensioner revisions, and the cooling system weaknesses specific to this car.

signals.title: Part of Engine Finders
signals.text: JaguarEngine.uk is part of the Engine Finders family, the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace — giving you the research depth of an independent authority with a vetted route to action when you need one.
